cards.front: Why split a program into multiple files?
cards.back:
Multiple files provide:
  • Better organization
  • Faster compilation (only changed files recompile)
  • Easier collaboration
  • Code reusability across projects

cards.text: To use a function defined in another file, you need a {{c1::forward declaration}}. These are typically placed in {{c2::header files}} with {{c3::.h}} or {{c4::.hpp}} extensions.

cards.front: What is the difference between .cpp and .h files?
cards.back: .cpp files contain function definitions (implementations). .h (header) files contain forward declarations and other interface information.

cards.text: To include a header file you wrote, use {{c1::#include "filename.h"}} with {{c2::quotes}}. For standard library headers, use {{c3::angle brackets}} like {{c4::<iostream>}}.

cards.question: If add.cpp defines add(), what should add.h contain?
cards.code:
// add.cpp
int add(int x, int y) {
return x + y;
}
cards.explanation: The header file should contain the forward declaration (prototype) of the function, not the definition.

cards.front: What is the difference between compiling and linking?
cards.back: Compiling converts each .cpp file to object code. Linking combines all object files into a single executable, resolving function calls across files.

cards.question: How would main.cpp use a function from math.cpp?
cards.code:
// math.cpp
int square(int x) { return x * x; }
cards.answer:
// math.h
int square(int x);

// main.cpp
#include "math.h"
int main() {
int result = square(5);
}
cards.explanation: Create math.h with the declaration, include it in main.cpp, and compile both .cpp files together.

cards.text: Header files should contain {{c1::declarations}} not {{c2::definitions}} (with some exceptions). Definitions belong in {{c3::.cpp}} files.

cards.front: Should a .cpp file include its own corresponding .h file?
cards.back: Yes. This ensures the implementation matches the declarations and catches errors where they don't match.

cards.concept: Best practices for organizing multi-file programs
cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines:
  • Group related functions in the same .cpp file
  • Create a corresponding .h file for each .cpp file
  • Use meaningful file names that describe the contents
  • Include only necessary headers
  • Add header guards to prevent multiple inclusion
examples: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

cards.front: Can a program have multiple .cpp files?
cards.back: Yes, a program can have many .cpp files. However, only ONE .cpp file can contain the main() function.
